behaviors.description: Clause extractor identifies all key terms (parties, dates, obligations, penalties) in the contract
behaviors.justification: The extractor is an AI component whose accuracy depends on the LLM's actual ability to recognize key terms in naturally-written legal language. Mocking the LLM call would hide the real failure mode: the model failing to extract terms when they appear in unexpected locations, non-standard formats, or embedded within other clauses. Only running with the real LLM can expose whether the extractor actually finds obligations mentioned indirectly or dates in unconventional formats.
behaviors.test_description: Execute the clause extractor with real LLM API calls on a diverse test set of contracts that vary in formatting, terminology, and clause structure. Include edge cases such as dates embedded in penalty clauses, parties mentioned only in signature blocks, and obligations expressed indirectly or with non-standard phrasing. Verify that all key term types are extracted correctly for each contract.

behaviors.description: Risk assessor flags problematic language, including indirect or euphemistic phrasing
behaviors.justification: Detecting indirect and euphemistic language (e.g., 'best efforts', 'subject to change') requires the LLM to understand nuanced semantics. Mocking the risk assessor to return 'flagged' hides the critical failure: the real model not recognizing risky language when it is expressed indirectly or using business-standard terms that should violate the policy. The test must use the actual LLM to verify it catches subtle phrasing that technically violates the risk policy.
behaviors.test_description: Run the risk assessor with real LLM API calls on contracts containing indirectly-expressed risky language that matches the configured risk policy but uses euphemisms, hedged language, or standard business terms. For example, test contracts with 'reasonable efforts' language when policy forbids vague obligations, or 'subject to change' when policy forbids unilateral modification rights. Verify that the assessor flags problematic language even when expressed obliquely without explicit policy violation keywords.

behaviors.description: Compliance checker correctly determines whether contract meets applicable regulatory requirements for the configured jurisdiction
behaviors.justification: As an 'AI reviewer' alongside the clause extractor and risk assessor, the compliance checker likely relies on an LLM to evaluate regulatory requirements. Real failures include: using incorrect jurisdiction rules, misapplying complex regulatory requirements, or confusing rules across jurisdictions. A mocked compliance function hides these failures. If the checker uses a rules database instead, Integration would be the minimum. However, given the architectural context, Agentic (LLM-based evaluation) is more probable. The test must use real evaluation logic to catch regulatory misinterpretations.
behaviors.test_description: Execute the compliance checker (whether LLM-based or rules-based) for multiple distinct jurisdictions, using contracts that both satisfy and violate jurisdiction-specific requirements. Include regulatory requirements that are easy to confuse across jurisdictions, and contracts with complex compliance edge cases. Verify correct compliance determination for each jurisdiction without cross-jurisdiction confusion.

behaviors.description: When reviewers conflict on the same clause, merge step preserves both assessments with correct attribution
behaviors.justification: The merge is pure deterministic logic operating on structured outputs from the three reviewers. Real failures are: findings disappearing, incorrect attribution, malformed conflict representation, or crashes on concurrent results. These can all be caught by unit testing with carefully constructed test data simulating conflicting assessments. No external I/O, no LLM calls, no concurrency dependencies — the merge function itself contains all the logic needed to reproduce failures. Mock reviewer outputs are sufficient because we are testing merge correctness, not reviewer behavior.
behaviors.test_description: Unit test the merge function with test data representing conflicting assessments from all three reviewers on the same clause (e.g., one extractor says 'parties: ABC Corp', another risk assessor says 'high risk', compliance checker says 'non-compliant'). Call merge() and verify that the output contains both assessments with clear attribution to each reviewer, properly formatted conflict representation, and no loss of findings from any reviewer.

behaviors.description: Full pipeline (dispatch to 3 parallel reviewers, collect results, merge) completes correctly for a real contract without losing any findings
behaviors.justification: This behavior explicitly involves three independent agentic steps (the AI reviewers) running in parallel, plus orchestration (dispatch, collect, merge). Real failures include: race conditions between concurrent reviewers, timeouts before all collectors complete, findings lost during collection before merge, or crashes under real LLM latency. Unit tests with mocked reviewers miss race conditions and real timing issues. Agentic tests (calling the pipeline with real reviewers) would likely catch most failures but do not explicitly validate the orchestration—parallelism, collection sequencing, and latency handling. Workflow-level testing is required to verify the pipeline correctly coordinates multiple agentic steps in parallel and handles the sequencing and concurrency correctly.
behaviors.test_description: Execute the complete pipeline end-to-end using a real contract document and the production LLM for all three reviewers. Launch all three reviewers in true parallel execution. Monitor that the collection step does not begin merge until all three reviewers return results, and verify that the final unified report contains findings from all three reviewers—clause extractions, risk flags, and compliance determinations—without loss, duplication, or corruption despite variable latency in real LLM API calls.
